notes: Extended from m7p/manifest.json. `date_confidence` = high|medium|unknown. `status` = published|draft. Topics use the closed vocabulary documented in scripts/topics.yml. Metadata fields populated from automated transcript research (Agent 1 + Agent 2); verify against recordings before publishing as distilled pages.
corrections_log: Saturday202602021morning.txt — filename date is a typo. Transcript content is the eigenvalue/characteristic-polynomial lesson which fits 2026-02-21, not 2026-02-02. Manifest dates it 2026-02-21 with note.
Saturday0404morning.txt and Saturday04062026afternoon1.txt are byte-identical (verified via diff -q). The latter is a mislabeled duplicate upload. Manifest records both; only one should be published.
video1426368535.txt content is closely parallel (but not byte-identical) to Saturday0404morning.txt — likely the parallel-class rerun, dated approximately 2026-04-04.
video1897151257.txt content places it during the conic→eigenvalue transition; inferred 2026-02-28 (low confidence).
video1112502915.txt is an individual session with Elaine on dot/cross product, references 'physics in summer in two weeks' — inferred late May 2026.

sessions.title_en: Fermat's Little Theorem, Euler Totient, and Cyclicity in Modular Arithmetic
sessions.lead: Proves Fermat's little theorem and Euler's totient theorem by tracking how multiplication by a permutes the reduced-residue set, then applies the φ-cyclicity to compute the last two digits of 7^2025 via Chinese Remainder Theorem.
key_theorems: Fermat's little: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p)
Euler's theorem: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) when gcd(a,n)=1
φ multiplicative formula via prime factors
Chinese Remainder Theorem
sessions.homework: Compute last three digits of 3^2025; prove that the n-th roots of unity sum to zero by symmetry (one-line proof).
sessions.notes: Heavy EN/ZH code-switching including long disciplinary monologue with parents about students not engaging with the 'why' of homework. One garbled OCR stretch (repeated 五的十次方).

sessions.title_en: Modular Arithmetic, Roots of Unity, and Symmetry Counts
sessions.lead: Reviews 7^2025 mod 1000 via binomial expansion + CRT, connects modular cyclicity to geometry of n-th roots of unity, and counts the symmetry orders of a square (8) and cube (48).
key_theorems: Σ(n-th roots of unity) = 0 by rotational symmetry
order of group element divides φ(n)
sessions.homework: Review AoPS Precalculus Ch 7 (roots-of-unity filter); no new problems assigned.
sessions.notes: Continuation of morning session. Heavy EN/ZH code-switching.

sessions.title_en: Proof of Euler's Formula via Additive and Multiplicative Functional Equations
sessions.lead: Builds a rigorous proof of e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ by classifying continuous additive functions on ℝ as linear, then bridging the multiplicative property of unit complex numbers to additivity through the logarithm.
key_theorems: Continuous additive functions on ℝ are linear: f(x) = kx
log(ab) = log a + log b
z(θ)·z(φ) = z(θ+φ) for unit-modulus complex numbers
e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ
sessions.homework: Determine k = i in z(θ) = e^(kθ) via infinitesimal expansion; prove angle-addition formulas via altitude construction on the unit circle.
sessions.notes: Substantial ε-δ continuity discussion. Toby and Lucas drive most of the derivation.

sessions.title_en: Solving cos θ = a and sin θ = a via Complex Exponentials
sessions.lead: Uses Euler's formula to write cos and sin as sums of complex exponentials, then solves cos θ = 2, sin θ = 7, and cos θ = 1+i by reducing each to a quadratic in e^(iθ) and inverting via natural log.
key_theorems: cos θ = (e^(iθ)+e^(-iθ))/2
sin θ = (e^(iθ)-e^(-iθ))/(2i)
General solution: θ = -i log(a ± √(a²-1))
sessions.homework: Derive general formulas for sin θ = a and cos θ = a for arbitrary (including complex) a; finish simplifying cos θ = 1+i.
sessions.notes: Long disciplinary discussion in Mandarin with parents about students (Eddie, Catherine, Holmes, Elaine) being too quiet when asked questions.

sessions.title_en: Hyperbola Geometry: Foci, Asymptotes, and a² + b² = c²
sessions.lead: Derives the focal-point relation c² = a² + b² for hyperbolas by multiple methods, including an asymptotic-limit argument that constructs a small similar triangle at infinity.
key_theorems: Hyperbola focal property: |d₁-d₂| = 2a
Hyperbola relation: c² = a²+b²
Asymptotes y = ±(b/a)x
Latus rectum coordinate b²/a
sessions.homework: Set up the parabola as the borderline between hyperbola and ellipse: locus of points equidistant from a focus and a directrix line.
sessions.notes: Toby suggests an elegant b → ib substitution turning the hyperbola equation into the ellipse equation — anticipates the next session.

sessions.title_en: Eccentricity Definition of Conics; Parabola as the Borderline Case
sessions.lead: Establishes the third equivalent definition of conics via ε = (distance to focus)/(distance to directrix), proving ε<1 ⇒ ellipse, ε>1 ⇒ hyperbola, ε=1 ⇒ parabola.
key_theorems: Ellipse focus-directrix ratio: PF/PL = c/a = ε < 1
Standard parabola from focus-directrix: x² = 4cy
All parabolas are similar
sessions.homework: Prove the focus-directrix ratio for hyperbolas; prove all parabolas are similar; show that not all cubic curves are similar.
sessions.notes: Repeated 'zero squared' OCR artifact. Heavy algebraic simplification involving |a - cx₀/a|.

sessions.title_en: Cramer's Rule, Cofactors, and Unique Parabola Through Three Points
sessions.lead: Motivates Cramer's rule from fitting a unique parabola through three non-collinear points; factors the Vandermonde-like determinant and constructs the matrix inverse from cofactors.
key_theorems: Vandermonde determinant factors as (x₁-x₂)(x₂-x₃)(x₃-x₁)
Three distinct x-coordinates uniquely determine an interpolating parabola
Cramer's rule: xᵢ = det(Mᵢ)/det(M)
M⁻¹ = (1/det M)·(cofactor matrixᵀ)
sessions.homework: Discussion continues in evening class.
sessions.notes: Worked example: 3×3 determinant with i (imaginary unit) entries = -10. Lucas asks if det(AB) = det(A)det(B); teacher confirms but defers proof.

sessions.title_en: Cramer's Rule Completed: Existence/Uniqueness Classification via Determinants
sessions.lead: Completes the morning's proof that M·adj(M) = det(M)·I by interpreting off-diagonal entries as determinants with repeated columns, then classifies M x = w solutions by (det M, det Mₓ).
key_theorems: M·adj(M) = det(M)·I
Classification: det M ≠ 0 unique; det M = 0 & det Mₓ ≠ 0 no solution; both zero infinite
sessions.homework: Organize notes; next session introduces interpolation as a shortcut.
sessions.notes: Worked example: 3×3 singular consistent system with infinite solutions. Long back-and-forth with Helms recalling the morning derivation.

sessions.title_en: Quadratic Form Classification of Conics via the Discriminant
sessions.lead: Reviews completing the square and the quadratic formula, then classifies ax² + bxy + cy² + dx + ey + g = 0 by b² - 4ac to distinguish ellipse, parabola, hyperbola.
key_theorems: Quadratic formula via completing the square
Vertex of y = ax²+bx+c at x = -b/(2a)
Discriminant test: b²-4ac < 0 ellipse, = 0 parabola, > 0 hyperbola
sessions.homework: Build an electronically searchable index of notes; prepare for monthly review-discussion class on AMC-style problems.
sessions.notes: Very long Mandarin discussion (~half the transcript) addressing parents about study habits, memory retention, and proposing monthly discussion sessions / Discord-style peer collaboration.

sessions.title_en: Factoring the Quadratic Form ax² + bxy + cy² to Find Asymptotes
sessions.lead: Factors the general conic's quadratic part as a(x - λ₁y + A)(x - λ₂y + B) and reduces the shift terms to a 2×2 linear system for A, B.
key_theorems: ax² + bxy + cy² = a(x - λ₁y)(x - λ₂y) with λᵢ from the quadratic formula
A + B = d, -(Bλ₁ + Aλ₂) = e/f reduce to 2×2 linear system
sessions.notes: Short transcript (~10 KB), ends mid-discussion when the 2×2 linear system for A, B has no solution.

sessions.title_en: Graphing Polynomials and Rational Functions: Snaking, Asymptotes, Long Division
sessions.lead: Develops the 'snaking' procedure for graphing polynomials with multi-root sign chart, then extends to rational functions via long division to extract slant asymptotes and analyze approach direction from remainder sign.
key_theorems: Snaking method for polynomial sign chart
Slant asymptote via polynomial long division
Remainder leading term controls above/below asymptote
sessions.homework: Reconcile why the plotted curve appears to cross the slant asymptote where the remainder has no real root (graphing-inaccuracy investigation).
sessions.notes: Heavy EN/ZH code-switching; long dialogue and scolding about students forgetting basic techniques. One garbled OCR stretch (repeated x²). Year 2025 inferred — verify with Drive createdTime.

sessions.title_en: Solving Cubic Equations: Cardano, Vieta, and Complex Cube Roots
sessions.lead: Solves u³ - 5u + 2 = 0 via the Cardano substitution u = α + β, reduces to a quadratic in α³, β³ by Vieta, then recovers all three real roots by complex cube roots with 120° rotations.
key_theorems: Completing the cube: u = x - b/(3a) removes the quadratic term
Cardano: u = α+β with 3αβ+p=0 reduces cubic to quadratic in (α³, β³)
Vieta: x²+ax+b ⇒ x₁+x₂=-a, x₁x₂=b
Cube root in polar form gives three roots spaced 120° apart
sessions.homework: Show all three real roots of the cubic are recovered by including coterminal 120° rotations when taking the complex cube root.
sessions.notes: Worked example: x³-6x²+7x+2=0 has root x=2; remaining roots 2±√5 recovered by polynomial long division and polar arithmetic.

sessions.title_en: Asymptotic Analysis: General Principle for Handling Infinitesimals
sessions.lead: Reviews horizontal vs slant asymptotes across power-comparison cases, then pushes the class to articulate a general principle about which infinitesimal terms may be discarded when the limit is a number vs a function.
key_theorems: Horizontal asymptote = ratio of leading coefficients when degrees equal
Slant asymptote requires long division — cannot drop sub-leading numerator terms
sessions.homework: Review the recording; explain back the general principle for handling infinitesimals.
sessions.notes: Teacher visibly frustrated when students restate polynomial-specific rules instead of the general infinity-handling philosophy.

sessions.title_en: Full Graph of a 7th/6th-Degree Rational Function
sessions.lead: Pulls together factored numerator/denominator (with complex-conjugate quadratics), long-division slant asymptote, and salient points to hand-sketch a rational function and verify on Desmos.
key_theorems: Procedure: factor → vertical asymptotes → long division for slant asymptote → x/y intercepts → intersections with asymptote → topology weaving
Four-case classification by numerator/denominator degree
sessions.homework: Internalize and own the full rational-function graphing procedure.
sessions.notes: Continuation of morning session; long phone interruption mid-class; Desmos screen-share debugging.

sessions.title_en: Tangent-Circle Chain (AMC 2015 #25): Gateway to Infinite Series and Binary
sessions.lead: Uses an AMC tangent-circles problem to introduce infinite series via self-similar generating sums, then factors 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x^(2^k)) and connects to binary representation uniqueness.
key_theorems: Distance between tangency points: s = 2√(Rr) for two circles tangent to a common line
Closed form of geometric series
Infinite product factorization via binary digits
Uniqueness of binary representation
sessions.homework: Determine whether C(211,143) is even or odd using a smart method (Kummer / Lucas style); don't just compute.
sessions.notes: Opens the sequences-and-series chapter; Lucas drives much of the discovery.

sessions.title_en: Riemann Zeta: ζ(1+ε) Bound and ζ(2) = π²/6 via sin(x)/x Vieta
sessions.lead: Finishes the convergence bound for Σ 1/n^(1+ε) via telescoping (with 1/ε leading coefficient), then derives ζ(2) = π²/6 by treating sin(√u)/√u as an infinite-degree polynomial and applying Vieta to its reciprocal.
key_theorems: Σ 1/n^(1+ε) bounded (telescoping with power-rule coefficient)
ζ(2) = π²/6 via Vieta on power-series of sin(√u)/√u
σ₋₁ = coefficient ratio from reciprocal polynomial
sessions.homework: Use the same technique to compute ζ(4).
sessions.notes: Long detour where Lucas describes 3Blue1Brown's lighthouse proof; teacher pivots to algebraic Vieta route.

sessions.title_en: Linear Recursions with Double Roots: Generating-Function Derivation of n·λ^(n-1)
sessions.lead: Re-derives why a double-root linear recursion requires an extra n·λ^n term (degree-of-freedom argument) and rigorously expands 1/(1-λx)² = Σ(n+1)λ^n x^n via the negative-binomial coefficient C(-2,k).
key_theorems: Double-root general solution: fₙ = (A + Bn)λ^n
Generating-function identity: Σ(n+1)x^n = 1/(1-x)²
sessions.homework: Build searchable inventory of past notes; monthly Overleaf submission.
sessions.notes: Very long transcript (~113 KB). New student Brayden introduced. Significant meta-discussion about AI/ChatGPT usage and personalized system instructions.

sessions.title_en: Double-Root Linear Recursion Completed; Generating-Function Identity 1/(1-λx)² = Σ(n+1)λⁿxⁿ
sessions.lead: Completes the double-root linear recursion: telescoping gives general solution f_n = (A+Bn)λⁿ; second derivation via generating functions proves 1/(1-u)² = Σ(n+1)uⁿ using the negative-power binomial.
key_theorems: Double-root general solution f_n = (A+Bn)λⁿ
Generating-function identity 1/(1-u)² = Σ(n+1)uⁿ
sessions.homework: Re-derive (28) by differentiating geometric series; generalise to 1/(1-u)^r (Lucas's Pascal-triangle conjecture); configure AI tool with stepwise-rigor instructions.
sessions.notes: Manifest title was wrong (claimed conic diagonalization); actual content is the recursion lesson — fixed. Last ~25% of recording is AI-collaboration meta-discussion rather than math.

sessions.title_en: Conic Metric Diagonalization Revisited: Trace/Det Invariance, Orthogonal Matrices, and the M·E = E·Λ Eigenvalue Setup
sessions.lead: Trace and determinant invariance under rotation proved by direct algebra (declining det(AB)=det(A)det(B)); eigenvalues recovered via Vieta on (λ₁+λ₂, λ₁λ₂) without finding θ; columns of R(θ) reinterpreted as eigenvectors yielding the matrix eigenvalue equation M·E = E·Λ.
key_theorems: Trace invariance under rotation
Determinant invariance under rotation (proved directly without det(AB)=det(A)det(B))
λ₁+λ₂ = a+c, λ₁λ₂ = ac-b² (Theorem 38)
Orthogonal matrix O^T O = I ⇒ O⁻¹ = O^T
M·E = E·Λ matrix eigenvalue equation
sessions.homework: Expand M·E = E·Λ column by column to get the per-vector eigenvalue equation M·εⱼ = λⱼ·εⱼ; verify O^T O = O O^T = I for R(θ); compute eigenvalues of [[4,-1],[-1,4]] via Theorem 38 then verify by finding θ.
sessions.notes: Manifest title was wrong (claimed det(M-λI)=0, which is actually the 2026-02-28 follow-up). Filename Saturday202602021morning.txt has extra-digit typo; date is 2026-02-21.
sessions._corrections: Filename typo: 202602021 → 2026-02-21; manifest title corrected (was 'Eigenvalue Equation det(M-λI)=0', that's the next session)

sessions.title_en: Skew-Axis Directions for Elliptical Conics via Complex-Rotated Asymptotes (y → yi)
sessions.lead: Extends the tan(2θ) = 2b/(a-c) symmetric-axis formula from hyperbolas to ellipses by reinterpreting bounding-box diagonal slopes as complex k₁,k₂ obtained by substituting yi for y.
key_theorems: tan(2θ) = 2b/(a-c) holds for both elliptical and hyperbolic conics
Box-diagonal interpretation of symmetric axes via yi substitution
sessions.homework: Solve 4x²+2xy+y²=4: find tan 2θ via quadratic in t=tan θ, then cos θ/sin θ via 1+t²=sec²θ, then U/V via rotation; verify against Desmos.
sessions.notes: Live ChatGPT screen-share comparing AI's matrix-heavy proof to the teacher's geometric shortcut; teaches prompt-engineering for rigor.

sessions.title_en: Cramer's Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
sessions.lead: Proves Cramer's rule from the cofactor adjugate formula, establishes the equivalence of left and right inverses via the column-upgrade trick, defines a linear vector space (closure under linear combination), and shows ker M is one.
key_theorems: M⁻¹ = (1/det M)·adj(M)
Cramer's rule: xⱼ = det(Mⱼ)/det(M)
MM⁻¹ = M⁻¹M = I for invertible M (two-sided inverse)
ker M is a linear subspace
Linear-combination closure characterizes vector spaces
sessions.homework: Prove that range(M) = {Mu : u ∈ ℝⁿ} is also a linear vector space using Toby's closure-check.
sessions.notes: Published as the gold-standard lesson template. Rich Socratic discussion with Toby/Lucas/Brayden. Teacher coins 'affine coordinates' for skewed basis.

sessions.title_en: Cofactor Inverse Formula Proven; Cramer's Rule Re-derived; Singular Dichotomy
sessions.lead: Review companion to the morning LVS session: walks Catherine through the off-diagonal proof of M·adj(M)/det M = I via the determinant-with-repeated-row construction, re-derives Cramer's rule from M^{-1}·f, and introduces the singular dichotomy theorem as homework.
key_theorems: M·adj(M)/det M = I (cofactor inverse formula, proved end-to-end including the off-diagonal case)
Cramer's rule x_j = det(M^(j←f))/det M
Singular dichotomy: det M=0 ⇒ either all det(M^(j←f))=0 or all ≠0; mixed outcome impossible
sessions.homework: Prove the singular dichotomy (Theorem 10): if det M=0, prove det(M^(j←f))=0 for all j, or det(M^(j←f))≠0 for all j — never mixed.
sessions.notes: Drive Last-Modified 2026-03-12; date may actually be 2026-03-12 not 2026-03-07. Companion to `2026-03-07-LVS`: that session imports the cofactor inverse formula; this session proves it. Heavy back-and-forth with Catherine relearning cofactor definition; Helms re-explains.

sessions.title_en: Gram Matrix VᵀV: Rank Tests, Sub-Volume Inequalities, Gauss-Jordan Inverse
sessions.lead: Uses Gram matrix VᵀV to test linear independence in high-dimensional space (sub-determinants for partial ranks), derives Toby's triangle-inequality constraints on off-diagonal cosines, and teaches Gauss-Jordan row reduction for inverse computation.
key_theorems: Rank via det(VᵀV) and principal minors
Mₖₖ cofactor ≥ det(M) for normalized Gram matrix (sub-volume bound)
T = U·V⁻¹ from images of basis
Gauss-Jordan elimination yields A⁻¹ alongside I
sessions.homework: Finish Gauss-Jordan inversion of the worked 3×3 example down to identity and verify by multiplying back.
sessions.notes: Long row-reduction example with nasty fractions.

sessions.title_en: Solving Linear Recursions by Diagonalizing the Companion Matrix; Nilpotent Intro
sessions.lead: Reformulates a second-order linear recursion as first-order vector recursion aₙ₊₁ = M·aₙ, solves by diagonalizing the companion matrix, and introduces the nilpotent block for repeated eigenvalue.
key_theorems: Companion matrix of linear recursion
M^n via V·Λ^n·V⁻¹
Repeated eigenvalue: generalized eigenvector v₂ with (M-λI)v₂ = v₁ → Jordan block λI+N
sessions.homework: Raise (λI+N)^n via binomial expansion and verify it reproduces (A+Bn)λ^n.
sessions.notes: Long meta-talk on monthly Overleaf note submissions as accountability mechanism. NOTE: byte-identical to `Saturday04062026afternoon1.txt` — duplicate upload; this is the canonical entry.

sessions.title_en: Powering Jordan Blocks: Binomial Expansion with Nilpotent N
sessions.lead: Continues from morning by explicitly powering (λI+N)^n for 2×2 and generalizing to k×k Jordan blocks, showing that the binomial expansion produces the polynomial-in-n coefficient predicted by the old algebraic theory.
key_theorems: Nilpotent shift: Nₖ^j moves ones to (j+1)-th super-diagonal; Nₖ^k = 0
(λI+N)^n = Σ C(n,j) λ^(n-j) N^j with only j<k terms surviving
k-fold root solution: aₙ = λ^n · (polynomial in n of degree k-1)
sessions.notes: Closes with rotational 2×2 having complex eigenvalues ±e^(iθ), motivating Pauli sigmas / complex eigenspaces next.

sessions.lead: Byte-identical to Saturday0404morning.txt — mislabeled upload.

sessions.title_en: Cayley–Hamilton via Cyclic Basis: Existence Gap and Four Proof Strategies
sessions.lead: Recaps the algebraic Cayley–Hamilton proof using (λI-M)·adj(λI-M) = det(λI-M)·I, then resumes the cyclic-basis proof, identifies the existence-of-cyclic-basis gap, and previews four distinct proof strategies.
key_theorems: Cayley–Hamilton: p(M) = 0 for p = char polynomial
Polynomials of one matrix commute
Characteristic-polynomial coefficients from cyclic-basis companion form
sessions.homework: Use AI to build meta-analysis notes connecting the four proofs; Helms to share his prompting workflow with the group.
sessions.notes: Long parent-teacher meeting (in Mandarin) interrupts the second half — extensive monologue on using AI for genuine learning vs passive copying.

sessions.title_en: Individual Session: Recovering Dot/Cross Product and Shadow-Area Interpretation
sessions.lead: One-on-one session reconstructing u·v = |u||v|cos θ and the cross product as a determinant with i,j,k symbols, then interpreting each component of u×v as the signed shadow area of the parallelogram on the corresponding coordinate plane.
key_theorems: u·v = u₁v₁+u₂v₂+u₃v₃ = |u||v|cos θ
|u×v|² = |u|²|v|² − (u·v)² (Lagrange identity)
Components of u×v are 2D shadow areas on yz, xz, xy planes
sessions.homework: Print AI-generated LaTeX notes, annotate by hand, rebuild the cross-product geometric proof three different ways.
sessions.notes: Individual session with Elaine. References 'physics in summer in two weeks' and Sophie possibly joining — date inferred late May 2026.

sessions.title_en: (Duplicate of 2026-01-24 morning)
sessions.lead: Byte-equivalent (or near) to Saturday20260124morning.txt. Agent's earlier classification ('Apr 4 Tuesday rerun') was wrong — verified by reading the transcript opening which matches Jan 24 verbatim.
sessions.notes: DUPLICATE: transcript opening matches Saturday20260124morning.txt verbatim. Marked excluded from index; preserves Drive video ID in case of independent value (different recording quality or angle).

sessions.title_en: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
sessions.lead: Formally derives (M-λI)v = 0 as the per-vector eigenvalue equation; det(M-λI) = 0 is the characteristic polynomial; Vieta gives Σλ=tr(M) and Πλ=det(M) for general n×n; the λ=0 trick gives the determinant identity in one line.
key_theorems: (M-λI)v = 0 per-vector eigenvalue equation (Theorem 42)
det(M-λI) = 0 characteristic polynomial criterion (Theorem 43)
Σλᵢ = tr(M), Πλᵢ = det(M) via Vieta (Theorem 44)
Constant-term-via-λ=0 trick for det identity
sessions.homework: Verify λ^(n-1) coefficient extraction for n=3; compute eigenvalues + eigenvectors of [[3,-2],[1,0]]; preview repeated-root case for next session.
sessions.notes: Small individual session with Catherine and Elaine. Crucial 'type-system intuition' moment when Catherine asks 'can I subtract a scalar from a matrix?'.
